observation: Immersive visuals cannot compensate for a weak or slowly advancing story.
decision: Complete and approve raw chronology and edited storyline before scripting or generating production footage.

observation: A standalone time-travel sequence adds duration without advancing the historical mission; the Chloe VS History reference begins directly in-world.
decision: End the host briefing with a spoken launch and hard cut directly into historical action; omit portals, countdowns and travel montages.
evidence: User feedback on EP01 opening structure

observation: Multiple historical protagonists can slow pacing when introduced as consecutive biographies.
decision: Introduce each historical figure at the moment their skill, choice or vulnerability becomes necessary; preserve their agency and avoid biography-card blocks.
evidence: User requested all four prisoners while maintaining Chloe-like pace

observation: Fast cuts alone do not guarantee retention; the story needs repeated conflict, false victories and escalating climaxes.
decision: Design 5–7 tension waves, vary conflict types, reveal a new cost within 10 seconds of apparent success and retain a documented aftershock after the main climax.

observation: Popular retellings can conflict with archival testimony about ownership, dates, locations and outcomes.
decision: Separate high-confidence facts, attributed recollections and unresolved claims in every raw story; keep contradictions visible rather than silently choosing the more dramatic version.
